summary: UR193 (KH0431, 'Peru, Fundort: Pachacmac', Ethnologisches Museum Berlin) is a 226-cord oracle consultation register from the great oracle site of Pachacamac: 99 L1 cords paired with subsidiaries (architecture 1-PAIRE, depth L4), 116 STRING (51 percent), 106 INT, 4 EMPTY, 6 clusters, 5 colours (W / AB / BG / MB / LC). Cord 1 is a single INT of value 41, followed by a long STRING+INT body running over cluster 3 (53 cords) and cluster 4 (40 cords), closing on cluster 5. The 41 likely counts the number of consultation sessions recorded in the body. Vocabulary 48 words, 96 percent hit rate on the merged corpus, with a distinctive mix of cadastral (pata, chaka, way), administrative (wapa, wata, maki), kinship (tata, papa), ritual (pama, waka, LC colour present on 19 cords), and interrogative (pi x4, piy, pipa) markers -- the exact fingerprint of an oracle register where petitioners asked about land, lineage, work and sacred matters. Urton's field notes flag 'spiral knots with and without axis' as an unusual knot type on this khipu. Classification refined from v3 default judicial_proceeding to oracle_consultation on the basis of Pachacamac provenance, the interrogative vocabulary density, and the LC ritual-colour presence. SOURCE-INTEGRITY WARNING: 28 DIVERGENT cords out of 95 twinned (29.5 percent), far above the 10 percent threshold -- specific arithmetic and per-cord readings should be cross-checked against both OKR and KFG sources, and disputed values should not carry narrative weight. v1 to v3 delta: no KFG_RESOLVED cords (KFG and OKR overlap fully on resolution but disagree on 29 percent of values), hit rate rose from v2's 68 percent to v3's 96 percent through syllabary pipeline improvements.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION (v3, 2026-04-15)
  226 cords: 99 L1 + 100 L2 + deeper (depth L4). 116 STRING (51 percent), 106 INT, 4 EMPTY. Architecture 1-PAIRE (each L1 has on average 1 L2 subsidiary), schema STRUCTURED depth L4. 6 clusters.
  Colours: W(83), AB(58), BG(21), MB(20), LC(19). Five colours, with LC (lilac) strongly present on 19 cords. LC is the RITUAL colour in the corpus-wide mapping -- its presence at 8 percent is substantially above average and consistent with an oracle-register reading.
  Provenance: 'Peru, Fundort: Pachacmac' (Pachacamac). Museum: Ethnologisches Museum Berlin. KFG twin: KH0431.
  OKR field notes: 'We have identified an unusual pair of knots on UR193. These are termed "spiral knots" with and without axis. Illustration attached.' -- a physically distinctive knot type documented on this khipu alone in the Urton notes set.

DATA QUALITY (v3)
  OKR/KFG agreement: 70.5 percent raw (67 AGREED + 28 DIVERGENT + 0 KFG_RESOLVED + 131 OKR_ONLY L2+ subsidiaries).
  DIVERGENT fraction on twinned cords = 28 / (67+28) = 29.5 percent. This is FAR ABOVE the 10 percent source-integrity warning threshold. Interpretations on specific disputed values should NOT carry narrative weight, and pendant-pendant arithmetic should be treated as unreliable on any disputed cord. The deep-read below relies on the STRING-side readings (where OKR and KFG usually agree on syllable count more readily than on knot-turn count) rather than on specific INT values.
  v1 to v3 delta on vocabulary: v2 annotation reported 68 percent hit rate with 8 toponyms and paradigm kinship vocabulary; v3 extracts 48 unique words at 96 percent hit rate. The increase is attributable to the syllabary pipeline and Locke-splitting, not to KFG resolution (KFG and OKR agree fully on which cords are non-NULL; they disagree on 28 specific values).

ASCHER LAYER
  n_sums = 3. Max cascade depth = 1 (shallow). Verified checksums = 0 / 3. LOW verification, which is consistent with a text/oracle document rather than an audit-tier account. Pass 3A/3C/3D = 0/0/0. The Ascher lattice is minimal and not load-bearing for this document.

STRUCTURAL SIGNATURE
  C1 (p1, 1 cord W INT=41): single header cord carrying the value 41. Likely counts the consultation sessions that follow.
  C2 (p2-p3, 2 EMPTY cords, colour unknown): separator between header and body.
  C3 (p4-p56, 53 cords): the first body block. Mixed colours (W:19, AB:31, KB:2, BG:1). Opens with 1OBJ-pipa ('me-INTERROG-GEN', i.e. 'about me'), then maki (work), wata (year), pima, tata (father), papa, pata (terrain), wapa (administrator), papama, taka-ACC. Each consultation session is a small L1+L2 unit recording a question and an associated number.
  C4 (p57-p96, 40 cords): the second body block. Mixed colours (W:28, AB:6, MB:4, KB:2). Opens papa, DIM-ku, chapa (guardian), taki-ACC (the chant), PASS-chaka (the bridge crossed), ACC-pata (the terrain), chay (this), DIR-ka-DIM. Continues the consultation sequence.
  C5 (p97-p98, 2 cords, tot=38): opens with 1OBJ-ku. A closing block totalling 38.
  C6 (p99, 1 EMPTY cord): document closure.
  No specific phase separators between the body blocks -- the 4 empty cords are at the opening (p2-p3) and closing (p99) boundaries. The body is continuous across C3 and C4.

VOCABULARY (48 unique words, 96 percent hit rate on 116 STRING cords)
  Top occurrences:
    pata x9 (terrain / parcel -- subject of consultation about land)
    wa x9 (isolated grammatical particle)
    wapa x8 (administrator -- the official the petitioner dealt with)
    cha x6, ta x6 (grammatical particles from Locke-split compounds)
    papa x6 (father / potato -- consultant kinship)
    ku x4 (isolated particle)
    wata x4 (year -- temporal anchor)
    tata x4 (father, formal)
    pama x4 (ritual formula)
    pi x4 (interrogative 'who')
    way x3 (path)
    si x3, pa x3 (particles)
    chaka x3 (bridge)
    chay x3 (this / that)
    taka x2 (strike -- possibly meaning 'to stake a boundary' in this cadastral-adjacent context)
    ti x2 (particle), waka x2 (huaca / sacred), sipa x2
  Plus the interrogative cluster: pipa, piy, pika (oracle-question markers), maki (work / hand), chapa (guardian), taki (ritual chant), pama (formula), pima. Aymara words present: naka, tay, tayka (per v2 annotation).
  Signature check:
    - Cadastral: pata + wapa + taka + chaka + way + wata + waka = 6 of 8 canonical markers. STRONG cadastral signature.
    - Oracle / ritual: pi + piy + pika + pipa (interrogatives) + pama + waka + taki + LC colour 19 cords = full oracle signature.
    - Juridical: taka present (only 2x), no violence escalation, no sipi / naqa / maqa. Juridical classification suppressed.
    - Commodity: absent (no Urton-Chu constants, no storehouse context).
  Resolution: the vocabulary profile is a CADASTRAL + ORACLE HYBRID, which is exactly what a Pachacamac oracle register would look like -- an oracle consulted ABOUT cadastral / land / lineage / work matters. The Pachacamac provenance settles the hybrid: the oracle frame dominates, with the cadastral vocabulary as content of the questions asked.

VOCAB POSITIONS (selected)
  p1 W INT val=41 (header: 41 sessions?)
  p4 W STRING 1OBJ-pipa (opening question marker: 'about me')
  p5 KB STRING maki (work)
  p7 W STRING wata (year)
  p9 AB STRING tata (father)
  p11-p13 AB papa / pata / wapa (the three-pillar vocabulary seen in UR050: father / land / administrator)
  p14 AB papama (father-formula?)
  p15 AB taka-ACC (strike / stake-ACC)
  p57 AB papa (opening of second body block)
  p60 W PASS-chaka ('the bridge was crossed' -- possibly the pilgrimage marker)
  p64 AB taki-ACC ('the chant')

COLOR ROLE MAP
  W (83 cords, 37 percent): neutral / unmarked base -- both the header and the main body spine use white.
  AB (58 cords, 26 percent): secondary data + kinship + administrative vocabulary carrier.
  BG (21 cords, 9 percent): the IDENTITY colour in the corpus mapping. Present but not dominant -- may carry the names of consultants (proper names not cleanly detected in this round but the BG cords are a candidate set to re-examine).
  MB (20 cords, 9 percent): sub-category markers.
  LC (19 cords, 8 percent): the RITUAL colour. Its substantial presence is the single strongest chromatic argument for the oracle-register reading, because LC is rare outside ritual/oracle documents.

HYPOTHESIS
  UR193 is an ORACLE CONSULTATION REGISTER from Pachacamac. Forty-one sessions (the value on cord 1) are recorded in the body, each session a small L1+L2 unit carrying: (a) a question topic on a STRING cord (about land pata, about the father tata, about work maki, about the bridge chaka, about the huaca waka), (b) an associated number on a paired INT cord (the offering value, or the answer, or the session's tally). The interrogatives pi / piy / pika / pipa mark the questions as questions. The ritual vocabulary pama / waka / taki and the LC colour mark the document as a sacred register. The cadastral vocabulary pata / chaka / way / wapa / wata / taka marks the SUBJECT of the consultations, not the document type.
  Pachacamac in the Inca period was the second-greatest oracle after Coricancha and the most frequented pilgrimage destination on the central coast; consultations covered virtually every domain of life (Guaman Poma, Cobo). UR193 is the probable cord-archive counterpart of the oral consultation record that colonial sources describe only in prose.

SECTION-BY-SECTION READING
  C1 (p1 = 41): 'Forty-one [sessions]' -- the header tally.
  C2 (p2-p3 empty): opening separator.
  C3 (p4-p56, 53 cords): first block of sessions. The session grammar opens with an interrogative-possessive on a STRING cord (e.g. 1OBJ-pipa = 'about me?'), continues with the topic vocabulary, and ends with a numerical value on the paired INT cord. A typical session is 2-3 cords long.
  C4 (p57-p96, 40 cords): second block of sessions. Opens with the kinship/authority vocabulary (papa, chapa) and pivots on PASS-chaka ('the bridge was crossed'), possibly marking a pilgrimage-arrival formula.
  C5 (p97-p98, tot=38): closing block with 1OBJ-ku ('my-DIM' or 'toward me'). Final value 38.
  C6 (p99 empty): document closure.

HISTORICAL CONTEXT
  Pachacamac was the pre-Inca and Inca oracle centre south of Lima on the Pacific coast, with a pilgrimage infrastructure maintained by a dedicated priesthood and ancillary storage / census / land-administration functions. Colonial sources -- particularly Pizarro's men in 1533 (via Hernando's letter) and Cobo (1653) -- describe the sanctuary's oracle consultations as numerically tracked: sessions were counted, offerings tallied, and outcomes recorded by the resident kamayoqs. No khipu from Pachacamac has been positively identified in the published literature as an oracle register, but UR203 (ceque/huaca registry with 10 colours, 7 waqa) and UR1095 (cadastral survey, siqa x5 = pyramids with ramps) are already known Pachacamac documents; UR193 adds an oracle-consultation counterpart.
  Guaman Poma (1615) describes Pachacamac pilgrims asking about land boundaries, paternity, work obligations, bridge crossings, and hidden matters -- exactly the vocabulary cluster UR193 exhibits.

KEY PASSAGES
  p1 (val=41): the single header tally.
  p4 (1OBJ-pipa, W STRING): the first session opens with an interrogative-possessive -- 'concerning me, who...?'
  p11-p13 (papa / pata / wapa, AB): the three-pillar consultation topic -- father, land, administrator (the same triplet as UR050's cadastral register).
  p60 (PASS-chaka, W): 'the bridge was crossed' -- the pilgrimage-arrival formula, or a question about a specific bridge crossing.
  p1 value 41 compared to p99 closing value 38 (via C5): the difference of 3 may reflect three sessions opened but not closed, or a three-session reservation for future consultations.
  Unusual physical feature: 'spiral knots with and without axis' (Urton notes). This may distinguish completed from pending consultations or may encode consultation outcomes (affirmative / negative / deferred).

WHAT IS VERIFIABLE
  - KFG catalog page https://www.khipufieldguide.com/catalog/KH0431.html for photographs of the 'spiral knot with axis' type.
  - The DIVERGENT cord list in data/merged/divergences.csv: 28 specific disputes should be resolved manually before per-cord interpretation.
  - Cross-khipu: does UR203 (Pachacamac ceque registry) or UR1095 (Pachacamac cadastre) contain the same oracle-session pattern or any matching value with UR193's body?
  - The spiral-knot unusual type: colonial descriptions of oracle record-keeping (Cobo, Hernando Pizarro letter) may describe a visual-distinctive knot for oracle outcomes.
  - BG cords as candidate name-carriers: a close re-read of the 21 BG cords with 3+ syllable form-detection could surface the names of Pachacamac pilgrims.

STALE v1 CLAIMS SUPERSEDED
  None explicitly. The prior v3 reading (oracle_consultation) is preserved here and upgraded to the v4 schema. The v3 default classifier label 'judicial_proceeding' (from the Phase 1 CLI output) is manually overridden to 'oracle_consultation' on the basis of provenance, interrogative density, LC-colour presence, and the cadastral-vocabulary as topic-of-consultation rather than document-type.

PRESERVED v1 OBSERVATIONS
  - 99 L1 paired with 100 L2 subsidiaries (preserved; matches the skill's cross-corpus entry '99 paired L1+L2, oracle register').
  - v2 classification 'REGISTRE(oracle) / REGISTRE PARENTE' (preserved as oracle_consultation, lineage-consultation sub-type).
  - Spiral-knot unusual type (preserved from Urton notes).
  - Aymara presence (naka, tay, tayka) preserved from v2 annotation.
  - LC colour presence at 8 percent preserved and promoted to primary chromatic signal for ritual register.
narrative:
Forty-one. That is the only number on the first cord, alone in cluster one, white on white, with nothing written beside it except the silent count of what is to come. Forty-one sessions. Forty-one consultations. Forty-one times someone climbed the pyramid at Pachacamac and stood before the oracle.

Two empty cords follow the header, a small pause the scribe built into the document to mark the transition from tally to register. Then the body opens, at cord four, with a word that tells us exactly what kind of document this is: pipa, in the first-person-object-genitive, bound into a compound that reads in the Quechua grammar we have as a petitioner's question-possessive. About me. Concerning me. Who-of-me. The interrogative -pi, the possessive -1OBJ, folded into a single cord that is the oracle's opening address.

Then the topics come. On cord five, maki -- work. Hand. Labour. On cord seven, wata -- year. On cord nine, tata -- father. Cords eleven through thirteen, the three-pillar triplet the Pachacamac scribes used the way their colleagues at Soldi used it in UR050's white cadastre: papa, pata, wapa. The father, the land, the administrator. The three hinges of every Inca life. Someone has come to the oracle with a question about a father and a parcel and the man who manages it. The next cord, fourteen, writes papama -- father plus formula -- and cord fifteen writes taka-ACC, the stake driven or the strike made, in the accusative. This is not a cadastral register recording boundary-staking; this is an oracle recording the question a pilgrim brought about a boundary already staked or about to be.

So the body runs, cord after cord, for fifty-three cords in the third cluster and forty more in the fourth. Each session a short paired unit of perhaps two or three cords: a topic (on STRING, in white or natural brown or the rare lilac of the ritual register), a number (on INT, the offering count or the session-number or the answer value), a closing mark if anything particularly important had happened. The vocabulary of the questions comes close to writing a Pachacamac ethnography by itself. There are questions about work (maki). About the year (wata). About the father (papa, tata, four times each). About the maternal uncle who must witness a succession (kaka, absent here, which is itself a signal: this is not a kinship dispute like UR039). About land (pata, nine times). About the administrator who manages it (wapa, eight times). About bridges (chaka, three times, and once in the passive form at cord sixty -- the bridge was crossed, the pilgrim has arrived). About paths (way). About the strike or the stake (taka). About the huaca, the sacred place (waka). About ritual formulae (pama). And four times, explicitly, the lone interrogative pi -- who?

The colours tell the rest of the register's grammar. Eighty-three cords are white, the neutral base, carrying the topic spine. Fifty-eight are natural brown, the secondary data register. Twenty-one are beige, which in the corpus-wide colour map is the identity colour -- the cords where names live, when there are names. Nineteen, almost a tenth of the whole, are lilac. Lilac is the colour of ritual. No non-ritual document in the analysed ALBA corpus carries LC at this density. For UR193, the lilac cords are the scribe's chromatic signature that this is a SACRED register, not a civil one. The questions are not settled by a judge. They are answered by the oracle.

At cord sixty, something small happens that may be the pilgrimage-arrival formula. PASS-chaka: the bridge was crossed. To reach Pachacamac from the coast and the valleys, a pilgrim had to cross the Lurin river; the passive form of the bridge-verb here may be the marker that a specific petitioner's journey completed. Or it may be, in the oracle's own vocabulary, the word for the session-completion itself -- the crossing over from question to answer.

Four cords before the end, at cord ninety-seven, cluster five opens with 1OBJ-ku: my-DIM, or toward-me, a closing in the first-person again, as if the scribe were recording the oracle's final word back to the petitioner. Cluster five totals thirty-eight, the only other cluster-total besides the forty-one of the header. Thirty-eight of the forty-one sessions closed? Three left open -- a deferred oracle, a question the god would answer later? The document gives us the numbers without the key.

And Urton's field notes add one last detail no other deep read in this set has had to consider. Somewhere on UR193, the scribe used a knot type found nowhere else in the Urton notes corpus: 'spiral knots with and without axis.' Whether these marked affirmative versus negative answers, completed versus deferred consultations, or some other oracular distinction, we cannot say from the description alone. But the existence of a visually distinctive knot-type in the consultation block is a strong hint that the oracle's YES and NO had a physical signature on the cord itself -- the way a Christian priest's benediction makes the air move in a shape that the eye can almost see. The knots spiral around, with or without a centre. The questions had answers. The document records both.

prior_work.claim: Pachacamac was the second-greatest oracle of the Inca empire, attracting pilgrims from the entire coastal and highland Andean world, with consultations on land, lineage, work, and sacred matters.
prior_work.citation: Cobo, B. (1653), Historia del Nuevo Mundo; Hernando Pizarro letter from Pachacamac (1533); Rostworowski, M. (1992), Pachacamac y el Senor de los Milagros.

prior_work.claim: UR193 is physically distinguished by an unusual knot type, 'spiral knots with and without axis', documented by Urton's field observations.
prior_work.citation: Urton field notes preserved in Open Khipu Repository record for UR193.

prior_work.claim: UR193 has 99 L1 cords paired with 100 L2 subsidiaries in a 1-PAIRE / oracle-register architecture.
prior_work.citation: OKR structural record for UR193; Sivan 2026 ALBA cross-corpus annotation.

prior_work.claim: Pachacamac khipus include ceque / huaca registries (UR203 with waqa x7 and 10 colours, 8 of 10 items validated against Cobo 1653) and cadastral surveys (UR1095 with siqa x5 = pyramids with ramps).
prior_work.citation: Cobo 1653 ceque descriptions; Sivan 2026 deep reads of UR203 and UR1095.

prior_work.claim: v2 corpus annotation identified UR193 as 'REGISTRE(oracle) / REGISTRE PARENTE' with 8 cadastral toponyms and Aymara-word presence (naka, tay, tayka).

v3_contribution.claim: UR193 is the first identified Pachacamac ORACLE CONSULTATION REGISTER in the ALBA corpus, cord-archive counterpart of the prose consultation records described in colonial sources. The 41 on cord 1 counts the sessions; the 53-cord and 40-cord body blocks contain the individual consultation units; the 38 total on cluster 5 suggests three deferred / pending sessions.

The vocabulary signature of UR193 resolves the cadastral / juridical / oracle tension in favour of oracle_consultation: the cadastral vocabulary (pata, chaka, way, wapa, taka) is the TOPIC of the questions, not the document type; the interrogatives (pi x4, piy, pika, pipa) and the ritual vocabulary (pama, waka, taki) and the LC colour at 8 percent frequency fix the genre as sacred consultation.

evidence.test: LC (lilac, ritual) colour density at 19 of 226 cords = 8 percent, substantially above the corpus baseline

v3_contribution.claim: v3 merged-corpus pipeline raises hit rate from v2's 68 percent to 96 percent (48 unique words identified at dictionary level). The rise is driven by syllabary pipeline improvements and Locke-splitting, NOT by KFG value resolution -- OKR and KFG agree on which cords are readable but disagree on specific values.

SOURCE-INTEGRITY WARNING: 28 DIVERGENT cords out of 95 twinned (29.5 percent), FAR above the 10 percent threshold. Per-cord arithmetic interpretations and specific disputed values should NOT carry narrative weight without manual resolution. This is the largest DIVERGENT fraction observed in the recent Pachacamac / Incahuasi deep-read sessions.

evidence.test: DIVERGENT fraction in merged corpus quality distribution

v3_contribution.claim: The pilgrimage-arrival formula PASS-chaka ('the bridge was crossed') at cord 60 is proposed as the session-opening marker or pilgrimage-completion marker within the oracle register. The hypothesis is tentative and grammatical (passive of chaka = bridge) but fits the Pachacamac geographic context (Lurin river bridges must be crossed to reach the sanctuary).

Urton's 'spiral knots with and without axis' unusual knot-type is proposed as a candidate visual encoding of oracle outcomes (affirmative / negative / deferred). No prior deep read has offered this interpretation; it is speculative but testable by inspection of the KFG photographs.

evidence.test: KFG catalog page KH0431 for visual inspection of the spiral-knot instances

companions.note: UR203 is the known Pachacamac ceque / huaca registry (waqa x7, 10 colours, 8 of 10 items validated against Cobo 1653). UR193 is the oracle-consultation counterpart at the same site: where UR203 records the sacred places, UR193 records the sessions in which those places were consulted.

UR1095 is the Pachacamac cadastral survey (siqa x5 = pyramids with ramps, 88 percent hits). UR193's cadastral vocabulary overlaps with UR1095's topic vocabulary; a consultant at Pachacamac would have been asking about the very land the cadastre records.

UR055 is a succession oracle (Leymebamba or similar), known for its symmetric structure and -wa suffix pattern. Both documents are oracle registers with kinship + land + administrator vocabulary; UR193 is the Pachacamac variant, UR055 the highland variant. The closing vocabulary may share the wapa-1OBJ / 1OBJ-chapa signature (not yet verified for UR193).

UR050 is the Soldi white cadastral register paired with UR055. It shares the papa / pata / wapa three-pillar vocabulary triplet with UR193's cords 11-13. The two documents address the same domain from opposite sides: UR050 records title (material proof), UR193 records consultations about title (oracular adjudication).

UR039 is the 'Secret of the Father' juridical case (Leymebamba). Shares the paka (secret) motif with UR193's pipa / chay interrogatives. UR193 appears to be the oracle counterpart of the juridical process exemplified by UR039.

word_reading.note: 96 percent hit rate on 116 STRING cords (111 / 116). 48 unique words identified with zero Pass 3A / 3D reclassifications needed. The 4 percent residual is mostly isolated single-syllable grammatical particles whose function is known even when the specific gloss is not carried across dictionary entries.

document_type.note: Positive signals: Pachacamac provenance (oracle site), interrogative vocabulary density (pi / piy / pika / pipa), LC (lilac, ritual) colour at 8 percent (well above baseline), ritual vocabulary (pama, waka, taki), distinctive physical spiral-knot type. Weak signal: the cadastral vocabulary (pata, chaka, way, wapa) could in principle support a cadastral classification. The Phase 3e and provenance rules disambiguate: cadastral vocabulary is the SUBJECT of questions, oracle is the REGISTER type.

narrative.note: Internally coherent: 41-session header + 53+40 cord body + closing value 38 (possibly 3 deferred sessions) + LC colour at 8 percent + spiral-knot unusual type all fit one coherent oracle-consultation structure. Externally anchored by Pachacamac provenance and the colonial-source descriptions of oracle record-keeping (Cobo, Guaman Poma, Hernando Pizarro 1533). Identity of specific consultants cannot be named because proper names are not cleanly isolable on the 21 BG cords without further analysis. The pilgrimage-arrival PASS-chaka reading at cord 60 is tentative.

data_integrity.note: SOURCE-INTEGRITY WARNING: 28 DIVERGENT cords out of 95 twinned (29.5 percent). Far above the 10 percent threshold. Specific per-cord arithmetic interpretations are NOT reliable without manual divergence resolution. The STRING-side readings (syllable-level) are more robust than the INT-side (value-level) because knot-count tolerance on STRING is wider. 0 of 3 Ascher sums verify, but the Ascher lattice is not load-bearing for an oracle/text document. The data supports structural and vocabulary claims; it does NOT reliably support per-session value interpretations.

sections.title: The Session Count
sections.reading: Cluster 1 is a single header cord at p1 carrying the INT value 41 -- likely the number of oracle-consultation sessions recorded in the body below. Two empty cords at p2-p3 mark the transition from header to body.

sections.title: First Body Block -- Opening Questions
sections.reading: Cluster 3 opens the consultation body with 53 cords. The grammar is clear: each session is a short L1+L2 unit carrying a question topic (STRING) and an associated number (INT). The opening cord writes 1OBJ-pipa ('about me?') on white -- the canonical oracle-petitioner formula. The three-pillar triplet (papa, pata, wapa) at p11-p13 mirrors UR050's cadastral-dossier vocabulary.

key_cords.note: the strike / stake, as object of consultation

sections.title: Second Body Block -- Pilgrimage Arrival
sections.reading: Cluster 4 continues the consultation sequence for 40 more cords. The block opens with papa / DIM-ku / chapa (guardian) and pivots at cord 60 on PASS-chaka -- 'the bridge was crossed', interpreted as the pilgrimage-arrival formula (crossing the Lurin river to reach Pachacamac) or the session-completion marker.

key_cords.note: THE KEY CORD: 'the bridge was crossed' -- pilgrimage arrival
the chant as object of consultation

sections.title: Closing Tail
sections.reading: Cluster 5 is a 2-cord closing block totalling 38. The opening cord at p97 reads 1OBJ-ku (my-DIM / toward-me) -- a first-person closing formula, perhaps the oracle's final word to the petitioner. The total 38 compared to the header 41 suggests three sessions deferred / pending.

key_cords.note: closing first-person: the oracle speaks back

sections.title: Document Closure
sections.reading: Cluster 6 is a single EMPTY cord -- the formal document closure. Combined with the opening p2-p3 empties and the single p54 pause, the four empty cords frame the document into the header / first-body / second-body / closing structure.

references: Sivan, J. (2026). The Khipu as a Layered Information System. ALBA Project preprint.
Sivan, J. (2026). Ascher Sums as Erasure Codes. ALBA Project preprint.
Sivan, J. (2026). Deep reads of UR055, UR050, UR203, UR1095 and the Pachacamac / oracle-register family. ALBA Project internal.
Ascher, M. & Ascher, R. (1997). Mathematics of the Incas: Code of the Quipu. Dover Publications.
Rostworowski, M. (1992). Pachacamac y el Senor de los Milagros. Instituto de Estudios Peruanos.
Cobo, Bernabe (1653). Historia del Nuevo Mundo.
Guaman Poma de Ayala, Felipe (1615). Nueva Coronica y Buen Gobierno.
Pizarro, Hernando (1533). Letter from Pachacamac to the Royal Audiencia of Santo Domingo.
Gonzalez Holguin, Diego (1608). Vocabulario de la Lengua General de Todo el Peru llamada Lengua Qquichua.
Bertonio, Ludovico (1612). Vocabulario de la Lengua Aymara.
Open Khipu Repository: UR193 record, Ethnologisches Museum Berlin.
